title: Reading and Use of English - Part 6
description: You are going to read a magazine article about an intellectual process known as critical thinking. For questions 31 – 36, choose the answer (A, B, C or D) which you think fits best according to the text. Mark your answers on the separate answer sheet.

content.title: The Impact of Remote Work on Urban Life

texts.A: [37]The dramatic and rapid mass migration of office workers to permanent remote working arrangements is proving to be a genuine death knell for the traditional city centre as a viable economic and social organism.[/37] [40]Without the predictable and substantial daily influx of office workers and their associated spending power, the entire carefully balanced ecosystem of small independent businesses—the neighbourhood cafés, specialist dry cleaners, newsagents, and sandwich bars—that evolved to depend entirely on that regular and reliable footfall is visibly and rapidly collapsing across major cities worldwide.[/40] The result is a troubling proliferation of ghost downtowns that are becoming progressively unsafe, economically stagnant, and psychologically dispiriting for the residents who remain. While the freedom of home working is enthusiastically celebrated by those fortunate enough to have comfortable domestic arrangements, such advocates conveniently overlook the irreplaceable loss of the genuine watercooler effect—the entirely spontaneous, unplanned collaboration and creative cross-pollination that demonstrably only occurs when diverse professionals share a common physical working space.
texts.B: [37]The gradual but accelerating decline of the traditional central business district is not an urban tragedy deserving public mourning but rather a long-overdue and genuinely exciting opportunity for fundamental urban renewal and creative reimagination of how cities serve their residents.[/37] For too many decades, our city centres have been designed entirely and single-mindedly around the narrow logistical needs of the daily commuter, a series of planning decisions that have consistently produced empty, lifeless streets at night, extortionate commercial and residential rents, and deeply unsatisfying urban environments for the humans who actually live there year-round. Remote work now presents a genuinely historic planning opportunity to convert these vast and increasingly underutilised office blocks into genuinely affordable and well-designed residential housing. [38]I broadly share the view that the urban commercial ecosystem is undergoing a profound transformation, but I believe the economic energy is simply migrating outward to suburban areas and smaller market towns, thereby revitalising local communities that have long been dismissed as mere bedroom suburbs with no distinct economic identity of their own.[/38]
texts.C: [39]I agree that the romanticized concept of spontaneous watercooler conversations generating organic innovation and breakthrough thinking is largely an idealized myth that conveniently overlooks the empirical reality that the majority of routine office work is, in practice, performed in individual isolation with headphones firmly in place and digital communication tools replacing face-to-face interaction.[/39] However, I share the genuine concern that remote work is inadvertently creating a new and troubling digital divide with serious spatial consequences for our existing cities. Those with sufficient financial resources can comfortably work from well-equipped private home offices in desirable residential locations, while essential service workers are still compelled to commute daily into increasingly hollow and poorly served urban centres. [38]The real and most pressing challenge for urban planners and policymakers is to ensure that any purported revitalization of smaller towns and suburbs driven by remote worker migration does not simply trigger a destructive new wave of gentrification that systematically displaces and marginalizes the existing working-class communities who have always lived and worked there.[/38]
texts.D: The extensive and often heated debate over remote work focuses excessively on the binary question of 'where' we physically perform our work while paying insufficient attention to the far more consequential question of how we choose to live our entire lives in a post-pandemic urban environment. Remote work represents perhaps the most powerful single environmental tool currently available to us; eliminating millions of daily commuter journeys removes staggering quantities of carbon emissions from our collective footprint with virtually no reduction in productivity. While I share the skepticism regarding alarmist declarations about the permanent 'death' of the city as a concept, [39]I believe our cities must consciously and deliberately adapt by reimagining themselves as vibrant '15-minute' hubs centred on leisure, culture, education, and human connection rather than mere engines of corporate productivity.[/39] [40]I fundamentally disagree with those who predict catastrophe for small businesses in city centres; those establishments that genuinely adapt to serve the new permanent resident population, rather than the transient commuter, will discover a more loyal and engaged customer base than they have ever had.[/40]

questions.question: Which expert has a different view from A on the fate of city centers in the age of remote work?
questions.explanation: Expert A sees a 'death knell' and 'ghost downtowns.' Expert B sees an 'opportunity for urban renewal' and a way to create affordable housing.

questions.question: Which expert shares B's opinion about the positive shift toward local community revitalization?
questions.explanation: Expert B speaks of 'revitalizing local communities.' Expert C also mentions the 'revitalization of smaller towns' (though they warn about gentrification).

questions.question: Which expert expresses a similar view to C regarding the myth of 'spontaneous collaboration' in offices?
questions.explanation: Expert C calls the 'watercooler effect' a 'romanticized concept.' Expert D similarly dismisses the traditional focus on the office as an 'engine of productivity,' prioritizing leisure and culture instead.

questions.question: Which expert has a different view from A on the impact of remote work on small businesses in the city center?
questions.explanation: Expert A says the ecosystem of small businesses is 'collapsing.' Expert D disagrees, stating that 'those that adapt... will thrive' and the city will become a place for residents.
